title: Cervical Cancer
oneLiner: A preventable HPV-driven malignancy of the cervix; screening saves lives, but late-stage disease carries high mortality.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 35-year-old woman presents to the gynaecology clinic with a 3-month history of postcoital bleeding and occasional intermenstrual spotting. She has had no cervical screening for 5 years. Speculum examination reveals a visible, friable lesion on the ectocervix that bleeds to touch. Bimanual exam suggests no parametrial involvement.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Postcoital bleeding** – classic sentinel symptom; suggests a lesion that is vascular and friable; blood vessels in tumour tissue are fragile.
- **Intermenstrual spotting** – extension of the same theme; any non-cyclical bleeding warrants investigation.
- **No screening for 5 years** – risk factor; regular screening catches pre-invasive disease (CIN) or early stage cancer.
- **Visible friable lesion** – advanced enough to be seen; squamous cell carcinoma often arises at the squamocolumnar junction.
- **Bleeds to touch** – friable tumour; this finding helps differentiate from cervical polyp (which is usually not friable) and endometriosis (no visible lesion).
- **No parametrial involvement** – suggests early stage (FIGO stage I or IIA); important for management planning.

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Asymptomatic** – found on routine smear with abnormal cytology (e.g., high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion → colposcopy → biopsy shows invasive cancer). Common in screening-engaged populations.
- **Persistent vaginal discharge** – foul-smelling or blood-tinged discharge that may be mistaken for pelvic inflammatory disease or cervicitis. The discharge results from tumour necrosis and secondary infection.
- **Pelvic pain / back pain / leg swelling** – advanced disease invading pelvic sidewall, sacral plexus, or compressing iliac vessels; often presents in women who have avoided screening or with aggressive histological variants (e.g., small cell neuroendocrine).
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Cervical cancer is a malignant neoplasm arising from the epithelium of the uterine cervix, most commonly at the transformation zone where squamous metaplasia occurs. It is almost always caused by persistent infection with high-risk human papillomavirus (HPV) types, especially 16 and 18.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** Fourth most common cancer in women worldwide; in many resource-limited settings it is the leading cause of cancer death among women.
- **Preventability:** Screening (cytology and/or HPV testing) and prophylactic vaccination dramatically reduce incidence. Yet many women are unscreened.
- **High stakes:** Early-stage disease can be cured (90% 5-year survival for stage I). Stage IV disease has <20% survival. This stark contrast drives the need for early detection.
- **Common pitfalls:** Postcoital bleeding is often dismissed by patients (and sometimes clinicians) as benign – always investigate. A normal-looking cervix does not exclude endocervical adenocarcinoma (which can be hidden in the canal).
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story:**
- Most cervical cancers originate at the **squamocolumnar junction** (transformation zone) – the area where the columnar epithelium of the endocervix meets the squamous epithelium of the ectocervix. This zone undergoes active metaplasia during puberty and pregnancy, making it vulnerable to HPV infection.
- **HPV** infects basal epithelial cells through microabrasions. The virus integrates into the host genome, and its **E6 and E7 oncoproteins** hijack the cell cycle:
  - **E6** binds and degrades **p53** (a tumour suppressor that normally triggers apoptosis or cell cycle arrest).
  - **E7** binds and degrades **pRb** (retinoblastoma protein), releasing E2F transcription factors and driving uncontrolled proliferation.
- This leads to a spectrum from **CIN (Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia) grades 1–3** to **invasive carcinoma**. CIN1 is often cleared, but persistent high-risk HPV infection with high-grade CIN (2/3) can progress over years (typically 10–15 years) to invasion.
- **Invasion** occurs when tumour cells breach the basement membrane and penetrate the underlying stroma. Lymphatic and vascular invasion follow.

**Why each key clinical sign happens:**
- **Postcoital bleeding** – The tumour is highly vascular and friable. Contact with the penis or speculum traumatises the abnormal vessels, leading to bleeding.
- **Vaginal discharge** – Necrotic tumour sloughs off and becomes infected; anaerobic bacteria produce foul odour.
- **Pelvic pain** – As tumour extends laterally, it invades the parametrial tissues, involving nerves and causing pain. Obstruction of the ureter can cause flank pain.
- **Leg oedema** – Compression of iliac veins or lymphatic channels by enlarged pelvic lymph nodes (or direct tumour mass).
- **Hydronephrosis** (asymptomatic until late) – Ureteric obstruction at the pelvic brim.
- **Weight loss, fatigue** – Paraneoplastic syndromes are rare; more often due to cachexia from advanced disease.

**Connection to pattern recognition cues:**
- The patient with postcoital bleeding and a visible friable lesion has an invasive tumour that has eroded the cervical surface.
- The asymptomatic patient had an abnormal smear because exfoliated dysplastic cells were shed into the vagina – even before invasion.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|---|---|---|
| **Infectious** | High-risk HPV (types 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, etc.) | Almost universal; “HPV positive” on cervical swab or HPV test |
| **Infectious cofactor** | HIV infection (immunosuppression) | “HIV positive” or low CD4 count; accelerates HPV progression |
| **Behavioural** | Early age at first intercourse, multiple sexual partners, high parity | History of sexual debut <16 years, >3 lifetime partners, 5+ children |
| **Environmental/iatrogenic** | Long-term oral contraceptive use | “Took the pill for 10 years” – weak association, but appears in stems |
| **Environmental** | Smoking (tobacco smoke carcinogens in cervical mucus) | Smoker; cigarettes per day × years |
| **Nutritional** | Low intake of antioxidants (vitamin C, E, folate) | Poor diet, low fruit/vegetable intake |
| **Pre-existing condition** | History of CIN or prior cervical cancer | “Previous treatment for CIN3” |
| **Vaccine preventable** | HPV vaccination status | “Unvaccinated” or “HPV vaccine declined” |

**Most common cause:** Persistent infection with high-risk HPV, especially type 16 (responsible for ~50% of cervical cancers worldwide).
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**
- **Definitive diagnosis** is made by **cervical biopsy** (colposcopy-guided or cone biopsy). Histology shows invasive squamous cell carcinoma (70–80%) or adenocarcinoma (20–25%).
- **Staging** is clinical (FIGO) but uses imaging to assess spread:
  - **FIGO stage I:** Confined to cervix.
  - **FIGO stage II:** Beyond cervix but not to pelvic sidewall or lower third vagina.
  - **FIGO stage III:** Extends to pelvic sidewall or lower third vagina, or causes hydronephrosis.
  - **FIGO stage IV:** Invades bladder/rectum or distant metastases.

**Key investigations:**

| Test | WHAT it shows | WHY you order it | HOW to interpret | Pattern-recognition clue |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cervical cytology (Pap smear)** | Abnormal squamous cells (ASCUS, LSIL, HSIL) | Screening; detects pre-invasive disease | HSIL + HPV 16/18 → high risk of CIN3/invasive cancer | A normal smear does not rule out adenocarcinoma; endocervical sampling needed. |
| **HPV DNA test** | Presence of high-risk HPV | Triage for ASCUS; primary screening | Positive for HPV 16/18 → high risk; negative → very low risk | Co-testing: cytology + HPV every 5 years is recommended. |
| **Colposcopy** | Visualise transformation zone, abnormal vascular patterns | Abnormal smear, visible lesion, or bleeding | Acetowhite epithelium, punctation, mosaicism → high-grade lesion | Biopsy of worst area; endocervical curettage if canal involved. |
| **Cervical biopsy** | Histology – invasion beyond basement membrane | Pathological confirmation | Squamous vs adenocarcinoma, depth of invasion, LVSI | “Invasive squamous cell carcinoma” is the confirmatory finding. |
| **MRI pelvis** | Tumour size, parametrial invasion, lymph nodes | Staging; plan treatment (surgery vs chemoRT) | Tumour diameter >4cm, parametrial involvement → stage IB2 or higher | A single finding that upstages: hydronephrosis on MRI = stage IIIB. |
| **PET-CT** | FDG-avid nodes and distant mets | Advanced disease; assess for recurrence | SUVmax; suspicious nodes outside pelvis → stage IVB | “Hot” para-aortic node changes radiation field. |
| **EUA + cystoscopy + proctoscopy** | Bladder/rectal invasion | If symptoms suggest IVA disease | Bulky tumour fixed to pelvic sidewall | Often omitted now due to MRI accuracy; still used in some settings. |

**What confirms the diagnosis:** Histology showing tumour cells invading cervical stroma.

**What rules out invasive cancer:** A colposcopically normal transformation zone with negative HPV test and normal cytology (very high negative predictive value).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Management depends on stage:**
- **Early stage (IA1 to IIA1):** Surgery (conization for microinvasive; radical hysterectomy with lymphadenectomy for IB-IIA).
- **Locally advanced (IB2-IVA):** Chemoradiation (cisplatin-based) ± brachytherapy.
- **Metastatic (IVB):** Palliative chemotherapy (cisplatin + paclitaxel ± bevacizumab) and symptom control.

**Immediate (first hour) — if bleeding:**
- **What:** Vaginal packing (if acute heavy bleeding) + IV access, cross-match.
- **Why:** Tumour bleeding can be brisk; packing tamponades the site.
- **Then:** Arrange urgent radiation or embolisation if unstable.
- **What NOT to do:** Do not clamp or cauterise blindly – can worsen bleeding.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**
- **Suspected early stage:**
  - Arrange colposcopy and biopsy if not done.
  - MRI pelvis to assess tumour size and parametria.
  - Bloods: FBC, U&E, LFT (baseline for chemotherapy), creatinine clearance (if chemo planned).
- **Advanced stage:**
  - Start hydration (to protect kidneys) if ureteric obstruction.
  - Place bilateral nephrostomies if creatinine rising and hydronephrosis.
  - Multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussion for treatment plan.
- **Pain management:** WHO ladder – NSAIDs + opioids if needed.

**Long-term:**

| Stage | First-line | Doses / Regimen | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| IA1 (no LVSI) | Cone biopsy (fertility sparing) | – | Low risk of recurrence; preserves fertility. |
| IA2–IB1 | Radical hysterectomy + pelvic lymphadenectomy | – | Removes primary + nodes; survival >90%. |
| IB2–IVA | Chemoradiation (external beam + brachytherapy) | **Cisplatin** 40 mg/m² IV weekly × 5–6 doses (concurrent) | Radiation sensitises tumour; cisplatin doubles locoregional control compared to RT alone. |
| IVB | Chemotherapy ± bevacizumab | **Cisplatin** 50 mg/m² + **Paclitaxel** 135 mg/m² (or carboplatin if renal impairment) IV q3wks + **Bevacizumab** 15 mg/kg q3wks | Bevacizumab improves OS in persistent/recurrent/metastatic disease (GOG 240). |

**What NOT to do:**
- **Do not perform simple hysterectomy** for invasive cancer (unless very early stage) – high risk of cutting through tumour and seeding.
- **Do not give chemotherapy without radiation** for locally advanced disease – surgery alone is insufficient for bulky tumours.
- **Do not forget fertility preservation** options (cone, trachelectomy) for young patients with stage IA1–IB1.
- **Do not use hormonal therapy** – cervical cancer is not hormone-sensitive.

**Follow-up:** Clinical exam + Pap smear every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months. Imaging if symptoms recur.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Cervical Cancer | Cervical Polyp | Endometrial Cancer (with cervical extension) | Cervicitis / STI (e.g., Chlamydia) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pain** | Late-stage pelvic pain; back pain | Usually none | Cramping if uterine involvement | Lower abdominal pain; dyspareunia |
| **Bleeding pattern** | Postcoital, intermenstrual, postmenopausal | Postcoital (often intermittent) | Postmenopausal (most common); menorrhagia | Postcoital (mucopurulent discharge) |
| **Speculum exam** | Friable, irregular, bleeds to touch | Smooth, pedunculated, non-friable | May see tumour protruding from os; often no visible cervical lesion | Red, oedematous cervix; mucopurulent discharge; friable but usually no mass |
| **Smear / HPV** | HSIL or high-risk HPV positive | Normal or ASCUS (if coexistent) | Normal (if endometrial cells present, may be suggestive) | Usually normal or ASCUS; can be HPV positive |
| **Colposcopy** | Acetowhite, abnormal vessels, mosaic | Normal | May see endometrial tissue or polyps; often normal | Colpitis, erythema; no atypia |
| **Key differentiator** | Biopsy shows invasive carcinoma | Biopsy shows benign polyp | Endometrial biopsy shows endometrial adenocarcinoma; MRI shows primary in endometrium | Swab positive for *Chlamydia trachomatis* or *Neisseria gonorrhoeae*; negative biopsy |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Early complications of missed diagnosis:**
- **Progression from CIN to invasive cancer** – missed screening or inadequate colposcopy. Signs: abnormal smear becomes HSIL, then invasion.
- **Pelvic lymph node metastasis** – occurs before symptoms; can cause leg oedema, hydronephrosis.

**Late-stage complications (treated or untreated):**
- **Ureteric obstruction → hydronephrosis → renal failure** – common cause of death. Present with flank pain, anuria, rising creatinine. **Do:** urgent nephrostomy.
- **Vesicovaginal or rectovaginal fistula** – due to tumour invasion or radiation necrosis. Presents as leaking urine or faeces per vagina. **Do:** urinary diversion or colostomy; surgery often delayed due to poor healing.
- **Pelvic sepsis (pyometra)** – if tumour blocks cervical os, causing infected collection. Presents with fever, pelvic pain, purulent discharge. **Do:** drainage.
- **Venous thromboembolism** – malignancy + pelvic mass + chemotherapy. **Do:** anticoagulate (LMWH).
- **Radiation complications** – proctitis, cystitis, vaginal stenosis; managed symptomatically.

**Late-presentation pattern (stem that tests "what if untreated"):**
- A 50-year-old smear-neglectful woman presents with **unilateral leg swelling** (lymphoedema from nodal involvement) and **flank pain** (hydronephrosis). CT shows a large cervical mass with pelvic sidewall invasion, para-aortic nodes, and hydronephrosis. FIGO stage IIIB – treated with chemoRT, but prognosis poor (5-year OS ~50% even with optimal therapy).
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **C.I.N. for HPV oncoproteins:**
   - **C** – E6 kills p**C**53 (p53)
   - **I** – E7 inactivates **I**-RB (pRb)
   - **N** – No cell cycle arrest → **N**eoplasia

2. **FIGO staging key boundaries (simplified):**
   - **I** – Inside cervix
   - **II** – Into vagina (upper 2/3) or parametria (not sidewall)
   - **III** – Sidewall involvement or lower vagina or hydronephrosis
   - **IV** – Invades mucosa of bladder/rectum or distant met

3. **Risk factors for persistent HPV:**
   - **S**moking, **E**arly coitus, **X** (multiple sexual partners), **U**nprotected sex, **S**uppressed immunity, **H**igh parity, **I**mmunosuppression (HIV) – "SEXUS HI" (not perfect but a hook)

**Clinical pearls:**
1. **Postcoital bleeding** is cervical cancer until proven otherwise – always perform speculum examination and arrange smear / colposcopy.
2. **Normal smear, but symptoms persist** → need colposcopy; endocervical adenocarcinoma can hide in the canal.
3. **HPV 16 and 18** cause ~70% of all cervical cancers – vaccination covers these two types.
4. **Concurrent chemoradiation** standard for locally advanced disease; cisplatin weekly is the protocol of choice.
5. **Fertility sparing surgery** (radical trachelectomy) is an option for stage IA1–IB1 patients with tumours <2cm.
6. **Hydronephrosis** equals FIGO stage IIIB regardless of other findings – this changes management from surgery to chemoRT.
7. **Bevacizumab** improves overall survival in advanced/recurrent disease by about 4 months, but increases risk of fistula and hypertension.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Ordering **only** a smear in a symptomatic patient – always do speculum and bimanual examination.
- Assuming a normal smear means no cancer – false negative rate is ~30% for adenocarcinoma.
- Performing a simple hysterectomy for CIN3 that may have occult invasion – leads to inadequate lymph node staging.
- Forgetting to check creatinine in a patient with advanced cervical cancer – impending renal failure is a common death cause.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**

| Situation | Trigger | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Acute massive vaginal bleeding** | Haemodynamic instability (HR >120, SBP <90), ongoing bleeding | Crash call; cross-match; vaginal packing; call gynae-oncology and interventional radiology |
| **Suspected tumour-associated septic shock** | Fever, hypotension, pelvic pain, purulent discharge | Sepsis 6; IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam); call ITU |
| **New unilateral leg swelling with pain** | Suspected DVT | Doppler ultrasound; start LMWH; call haematology if anticoagulation contraindicated |
| **Rising creatinine with oliguria** | Cr >300, UOP <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6 hours | Nephrostomy assessment; call urology and renal team |
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5 or any single red parameter** | RR ≤8 or ≥25; SpO2 ≤91%; HR ≤40 or ≥130; SBP ≤90; GCS <15 | Immediate review by senior nurse and FY3/med reg; escalate to critical care outreach |
| **GCS drop with suspicion of brain metastases** (rare) | GCS <13, unequal pupils | Crash call; CT head; involve neuro-oncology |
| **Severe chemotherapy side effect** (febrile neutropaenia) | Neutrophils <0.5, temperature >38°C | Start piperacillin-tazobactam; SEPSIS 6; call oncology and ITU if unwell |

**General rule:** If the patient looks sick, escalate early. Cervical cancer patients can deteriorate quickly from bleeding, sepsis, or obstruction. Always involve your registrar and the gynae-oncology team.

rawMarkdown:
## Cervical Cancer

**One-liner:** A preventable HPV-driven malignancy of the cervix; screening saves lives, but late-stage disease carries high mortality.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:** A 35-year-old woman presents to the gynaecology clinic with a 3-month history of postcoital bleeding and occasional intermenstrual spotting. She has had no cervical screening for 5 years. Speculum examination reveals a visible, friable lesion on the ectocervix that bleeds to touch. Bimanual exam suggests no parametrial involvement.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Postcoital bleeding** – classic sentinel symptom; suggests a lesion that is vascular and friable; blood vessels in tumour tissue are fragile.
- **Intermenstrual spotting** – extension of the same theme; any non-cyclical bleeding warrants investigation.
- **No screening for 5 years** – risk factor; regular screening catches pre-invasive disease (CIN) or early stage cancer.
- **Visible friable lesion** – advanced enough to be seen; squamous cell carcinoma often arises at the squamocolumnar junction.
- **Bleeds to touch** – friable tumour; this finding helps differentiate from cervical polyp (which is usually not friable) and endometriosis (no visible lesion).
- **No parametrial involvement** – suggests early stage (FIGO stage I or IIA); important for management planning.

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Asymptomatic** – found on routine smear with abnormal cytology (e.g., high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion → colposcopy → biopsy shows invasive cancer). Common in screening-engaged populations.
- **Persistent vaginal discharge** – foul-smelling or blood-tinged discharge that may be mistaken for pelvic inflammatory disease or cervicitis. The discharge results from tumour necrosis and secondary infection.
- **Pelvic pain / back pain / leg swelling** – advanced disease invading pelvic sidewall, sacral plexus, or compressing iliac vessels; often presents in women who have avoided screening or with aggressive histological variants (e.g., small cell neuroendocrine).

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Cervical cancer is a malignant neoplasm arising from the epithelium of the uterine cervix, most commonly at the transformation zone where squamous metaplasia occurs. It is almost always caused by persistent infection with high-risk human papillomavirus (HPV) types, especially 16 and 18.

**Why it matters:**
- **Frequency:** Fourth most common cancer in women worldwide; in many resource-limited settings it is the leading cause of cancer death among women.
- **Preventability:** Screening (cytology and/or HPV testing) and prophylactic vaccination dramatically reduce incidence. Yet many women are unscreened.
- **High stakes:** Early-stage disease can be cured (90% 5-year survival for stage I). Stage IV disease has <20% survival. This stark contrast drives the need for early detection.
- **Common pitfalls:** Postcoital bleeding is often dismissed by patients (and sometimes clinicians) as benign – always investigate. A normal-looking cervix does not exclude endocervical adenocarcinoma (which can be hidden in the canal).

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story:**
- Most cervical cancers originate at the **squamocolumnar junction** (transformation zone) – the area where the columnar epithelium of the endocervix meets the squamous epithelium of the ectocervix. This zone undergoes active metaplasia during puberty and pregnancy, making it vulnerable to HPV infection.
- **HPV** infects basal epithelial cells through microabrasions. The virus integrates into the host genome, and its **E6 and E7 oncoproteins** hijack the cell cycle:
  - **E6** binds and degrades **p53** (a tumour suppressor that normally triggers apoptosis or cell cycle arrest).
  - **E7** binds and degrades **pRb** (retinoblastoma protein), releasing E2F transcription factors and driving uncontrolled proliferation.
- This leads to a spectrum from **CIN (Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia) grades 1–3** to **invasive carcinoma**. CIN1 is often cleared, but persistent high-risk HPV infection with high-grade CIN (2/3) can progress over years (typically 10–15 years) to invasion.
- **Invasion** occurs when tumour cells breach the basement membrane and penetrate the underlying stroma. Lymphatic and vascular invasion follow.

**Why each key clinical sign happens:**
- **Postcoital bleeding** – The tumour is highly vascular and friable. Contact with the penis or speculum traumatises the abnormal vessels, leading to bleeding.
- **Vaginal discharge** – Necrotic tumour sloughs off and becomes infected; anaerobic bacteria produce foul odour.
- **Pelvic pain** – As tumour extends laterally, it invades the parametrial tissues, involving nerves and causing pain. Obstruction of the ureter can cause flank pain.
- **Leg oedema** – Compression of iliac veins or lymphatic channels by enlarged pelvic lymph nodes (or direct tumour mass).
- **Hydronephrosis** (asymptomatic until late) – Ureteric obstruction at the pelvic brim.
- **Weight loss, fatigue** – Paraneoplastic syndromes are rare; more often due to cachexia from advanced disease.

**Connection to pattern recognition cues:**
- The patient with postcoital bleeding and a visible friable lesion has an invasive tumour that has eroded the cervical surface.
- The asymptomatic patient had an abnormal smear because exfoliated dysplastic cells were shed into the vagina – even before invasion.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem clue |
|---|---|---|
| **Infectious** | High-risk HPV (types 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, etc.) | Almost universal; “HPV positive” on cervical swab or HPV test |
| **Infectious cofactor** | HIV infection (immunosuppression) | “HIV positive” or low CD4 count; accelerates HPV progression |
| **Behavioural** | Early age at first intercourse, multiple sexual partners, high parity | History of sexual debut <16 years, >3 lifetime partners, 5+ children |
| **Environmental/iatrogenic** | Long-term oral contraceptive use | “Took the pill for 10 years” – weak association, but appears in stems |
| **Environmental** | Smoking (tobacco smoke carcinogens in cervical mucus) | Smoker; cigarettes per day × years |
| **Nutritional** | Low intake of antioxidants (vitamin C, E, folate) | Poor diet, low fruit/vegetable intake |
| **Pre-existing condition** | History of CIN or prior cervical cancer | “Previous treatment for CIN3” |
| **Vaccine preventable** | HPV vaccination status | “Unvaccinated” or “HPV vaccine declined” |

**Most common cause:** Persistent infection with high-risk HPV, especially type 16 (responsible for ~50% of cervical cancers worldwide).

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:**
- **Definitive diagnosis** is made by **cervical biopsy** (colposcopy-guided or cone biopsy). Histology shows invasive squamous cell carcinoma (70–80%) or adenocarcinoma (20–25%).
- **Staging** is clinical (FIGO) but uses imaging to assess spread:
  - **FIGO stage I:** Confined to cervix.
  - **FIGO stage II:** Beyond cervix but not to pelvic sidewall or lower third vagina.
  - **FIGO stage III:** Extends to pelvic sidewall or lower third vagina, or causes hydronephrosis.
  - **FIGO stage IV:** Invades bladder/rectum or distant metastases.

**Key investigations:**

| Test | WHAT it shows | WHY you order it | HOW to interpret | Pattern-recognition clue |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cervical cytology (Pap smear)** | Abnormal squamous cells (ASCUS, LSIL, HSIL) | Screening; detects pre-invasive disease | HSIL + HPV 16/18 → high risk of CIN3/invasive cancer | A normal smear does not rule out adenocarcinoma; endocervical sampling needed. |
| **HPV DNA test** | Presence of high-risk HPV | Triage for ASCUS; primary screening | Positive for HPV 16/18 → high risk; negative → very low risk | Co-testing: cytology + HPV every 5 years is recommended. |
| **Colposcopy** | Visualise transformation zone, abnormal vascular patterns | Abnormal smear, visible lesion, or bleeding | Acetowhite epithelium, punctation, mosaicism → high-grade lesion | Biopsy of worst area; endocervical curettage if canal involved. |
| **Cervical biopsy** | Histology – invasion beyond basement membrane | Pathological confirmation | Squamous vs adenocarcinoma, depth of invasion, LVSI | “Invasive squamous cell carcinoma” is the confirmatory finding. |
| **MRI pelvis** | Tumour size, parametrial invasion, lymph nodes | Staging; plan treatment (surgery vs chemoRT) | Tumour diameter >4cm, parametrial involvement → stage IB2 or higher | A single finding that upstages: hydronephrosis on MRI = stage IIIB. |
| **PET-CT** | FDG-avid nodes and distant mets | Advanced disease; assess for recurrence | SUVmax; suspicious nodes outside pelvis → stage IVB | “Hot” para-aortic node changes radiation field. |
| **EUA + cystoscopy + proctoscopy** | Bladder/rectal invasion | If symptoms suggest IVA disease | Bulky tumour fixed to pelvic sidewall | Often omitted now due to MRI accuracy; still used in some settings. |

**What confirms the diagnosis:** Histology showing tumour cells invading cervical stroma.

**What rules out invasive cancer:** A colposcopically normal transformation zone with negative HPV test and normal cytology (very high negative predictive value).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Management depends on stage:**
- **Early stage (IA1 to IIA1):** Surgery (conization for microinvasive; radical hysterectomy with lymphadenectomy for IB-IIA).
- **Locally advanced (IB2-IVA):** Chemoradiation (cisplatin-based) ± brachytherapy.
- **Metastatic (IVB):** Palliative chemotherapy (cisplatin + paclitaxel ± bevacizumab) and symptom control.

**Immediate (first hour) — if bleeding:**
- **What:** Vaginal packing (if acute heavy bleeding) + IV access, cross-match.
- **Why:** Tumour bleeding can be brisk; packing tamponades the site.
- **Then:** Arrange urgent radiation or embolisation if unstable.
- **What NOT to do:** Do not clamp or cauterise blindly – can worsen bleeding.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**
- **Suspected early stage:**
  - Arrange colposcopy and biopsy if not done.
  - MRI pelvis to assess tumour size and parametria.
  - Bloods: FBC, U&E, LFT (baseline for chemotherapy), creatinine clearance (if chemo planned).
- **Advanced stage:**
  - Start hydration (to protect kidneys) if ureteric obstruction.
  - Place bilateral nephrostomies if creatinine rising and hydronephrosis.
  - Multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussion for treatment plan.
- **Pain management:** WHO ladder – NSAIDs + opioids if needed.

**Long-term:**

| Stage | First-line | Doses / Regimen | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| IA1 (no LVSI) | Cone biopsy (fertility sparing) | – | Low risk of recurrence; preserves fertility. |
| IA2–IB1 | Radical hysterectomy + pelvic lymphadenectomy | – | Removes primary + nodes; survival >90%. |
| IB2–IVA | Chemoradiation (external beam + brachytherapy) | **Cisplatin** 40 mg/m² IV weekly × 5–6 doses (concurrent) | Radiation sensitises tumour; cisplatin doubles locoregional control compared to RT alone. |
| IVB | Chemotherapy ± bevacizumab | **Cisplatin** 50 mg/m² + **Paclitaxel** 135 mg/m² (or carboplatin if renal impairment) IV q3wks + **Bevacizumab** 15 mg/kg q3wks | Bevacizumab improves OS in persistent/recurrent/metastatic disease (GOG 240). |

**What NOT to do:**
- **Do not perform simple hysterectomy** for invasive cancer (unless very early stage) – high risk of cutting through tumour and seeding.
- **Do not give chemotherapy without radiation** for locally advanced disease – surgery alone is insufficient for bulky tumours.
- **Do not forget fertility preservation** options (cone, trachelectomy) for young patients with stage IA1–IB1.
- **Do not use hormonal therapy** – cervical cancer is not hormone-sensitive.

**Follow-up:** Clinical exam + Pap smear every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months. Imaging if symptoms recur.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Cervical Cancer | Cervical Polyp | Endometrial Cancer (with cervical extension) | Cervicitis / STI (e.g., Chlamydia) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pain** | Late-stage pelvic pain; back pain | Usually none | Cramping if uterine involvement | Lower abdominal pain; dyspareunia |
| **Bleeding pattern** | Postcoital, intermenstrual, postmenopausal | Postcoital (often intermittent) | Postmenopausal (most common); menorrhagia | Postcoital (mucopurulent discharge) |
| **Speculum exam** | Friable, irregular, bleeds to touch | Smooth, pedunculated, non-friable | May see tumour protruding from os; often no visible cervical lesion | Red, oedematous cervix; mucopurulent discharge; friable but usually no mass |
| **Smear / HPV** | HSIL or high-risk HPV positive | Normal or ASCUS (if coexistent) | Normal (if endometrial cells present, may be suggestive) | Usually normal or ASCUS; can be HPV positive |
| **Colposcopy** | Acetowhite, abnormal vessels, mosaic | Normal | May see endometrial tissue or polyps; often normal | Colpitis, erythema; no atypia |
| **Key differentiator** | Biopsy shows invasive carcinoma | Biopsy shows benign polyp | Endometrial biopsy shows endometrial adenocarcinoma; MRI shows primary in endometrium | Swab positive for *Chlamydia trachomatis* or *Neisseria gonorrhoeae*; negative biopsy |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Early complications of missed diagnosis:**
- **Progression from CIN to invasive cancer** – missed screening or inadequate colposcopy. Signs: abnormal smear becomes HSIL, then invasion.
- **Pelvic lymph node metastasis** – occurs before symptoms; can cause leg oedema, hydronephrosis.

**Late-stage complications (treated or untreated):**
- **Ureteric obstruction → hydronephrosis → renal failure** – common cause of death. Present with flank pain, anuria, rising creatinine. **Do:** urgent nephrostomy.
- **Vesicovaginal or rectovaginal fistula** – due to tumour invasion or radiation necrosis. Presents as leaking urine or faeces per vagina. **Do:** urinary diversion or colostomy; surgery often delayed due to poor healing.
- **Pelvic sepsis (pyometra)** – if tumour blocks cervical os, causing infected collection. Presents with fever, pelvic pain, purulent discharge. **Do:** drainage.
- **Venous thromboembolism** – malignancy + pelvic mass + chemotherapy. **Do:** anticoagulate (LMWH).
- **Radiation complications** – proctitis, cystitis, vaginal stenosis; managed symptomatically.

**Late-presentation pattern (stem that tests "what if untreated"):**
- A 50-year-old smear-neglectful woman presents with **unilateral leg swelling** (lymphoedema from nodal involvement) and **flank pain** (hydronephrosis). CT shows a large cervical mass with pelvic sidewall invasion, para-aortic nodes, and hydronephrosis. FIGO stage IIIB – treated with chemoRT, but prognosis poor (5-year OS ~50% even with optimal therapy).

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **C.I.N. for HPV oncoproteins:**
   - **C** – E6 kills p**C**53 (p53)
   - **I** – E7 inactivates **I**-RB (pRb)
   - **N** – No cell cycle arrest → **N**eoplasia

2. **FIGO staging key boundaries (simplified):**
   - **I** – Inside cervix
   - **II** – Into vagina (upper 2/3) or parametria (not sidewall)
   - **III** – Sidewall involvement or lower vagina or hydronephrosis
   - **IV** – Invades mucosa of bladder/rectum or distant met

3. **Risk factors for persistent HPV:**
   - **S**moking, **E**arly coitus, **X** (multiple sexual partners), **U**nprotected sex, **S**uppressed immunity, **H**igh parity, **I**mmunosuppression (HIV) – "SEXUS HI" (not perfect but a hook)

**Clinical pearls:**
1. **Postcoital bleeding** is cervical cancer until proven otherwise – always perform speculum examination and arrange smear / colposcopy.
2. **Normal smear, but symptoms persist** → need colposcopy; endocervical adenocarcinoma can hide in the canal.
3. **HPV 16 and 18** cause ~70% of all cervical cancers – vaccination covers these two types.
4. **Concurrent chemoradiation** standard for locally advanced disease; cisplatin weekly is the protocol of choice.
5. **Fertility sparing surgery** (radical trachelectomy) is an option for stage IA1–IB1 patients with tumours <2cm.
6. **Hydronephrosis** equals FIGO stage IIIB regardless of other findings – this changes management from surgery to chemoRT.
7. **Bevacizumab** improves overall survival in advanced/recurrent disease by about 4 months, but increases risk of fistula and hypertension.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Ordering **only** a smear in a symptomatic patient – always do speculum and bimanual examination.
- Assuming a normal smear means no cancer – false negative rate is ~30% for adenocarcinoma.
- Performing a simple hysterectomy for CIN3 that may have occult invasion – leads to inadequate lymph node staging.
- Forgetting to check creatinine in a patient with advanced cervical cancer – impending renal failure is a common death cause.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**

| Situation | Trigger | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Acute massive vaginal bleeding** | Haemodynamic instability (HR >120, SBP <90), ongoing bleeding | Crash call; cross-match; vaginal packing; call gynae-oncology and interventional radiology |
| **Suspected tumour-associated septic shock** | Fever, hypotension, pelvic pain, purulent discharge | Sepsis 6; IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam); call ITU |
| **New unilateral leg swelling with pain** | Suspected DVT | Doppler ultrasound; start LMWH; call haematology if anticoagulation contraindicated |
| **Rising creatinine with oliguria** | Cr >300, UOP <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6 hours | Nephrostomy assessment; call urology and renal team |
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5 or any single red parameter** | RR ≤8 or ≥25; SpO2 ≤91%; HR ≤40 or ≥130; SBP ≤90; GCS <15 | Immediate review by senior nurse and FY3/med reg; escalate to critical care outreach |
| **GCS drop with suspicion of brain metastases** (rare) | GCS <13, unequal pupils | Crash call; CT head; involve neuro-oncology |
| **Severe chemotherapy side effect** (febrile neutropaenia) | Neutrophils <0.5, temperature >38°C | Start piperacillin-tazobactam; SEPSIS 6; call oncology and ITU if unwell |

**General rule:** If the patient looks sick, escalate early. Cervical cancer patients can deteriorate quickly from bleeding, sepsis, or obstruction. Always involve your registrar and the gynae-oncology team.
